rationale: This plan is stable and efficient, but the low spatial resolution (Nx=50) and second-order accuracy limit its ability to resolve wave propagation accurately for the linearized shallow-water equations. Suitable for coarse tests, but not for high-accuracy needs.

High spatial (Nx=200, 4th order) and temporal (RK4) accuracy with stable explicit time stepping make this plan well-suited for the problem. The computational cost is higher but justified by the accuracy gain for smooth, periodic solutions.

Crank-Nicolson is unconditionally stable and efficient (low cost), but the second-order accuracy and moderate resolution (Nx=100) are less optimal than high-order explicit or spectral methods for this smooth, non-stiff, periodic problem.
Fourier spectral methods are ideal for smooth, periodic, linear problems, and RK4 provides high temporal accuracy. The plan balances accuracy and efficiency, making it the most suitable for this PDE.

While stable and efficient, backward Euler is only first-order accurate in time and introduces excessive numerical damping, which is undesirable for wave propagation. Spectral space discretization is good, but time accuracy is limiting.

FEM with Crank-Nicolson is robust and stable, but for this smooth, periodic, linear problem, it is less efficient and less accurate than spectral or high-order finite difference approaches. Still reasonable for moderate accuracy needs.
Stable and efficient, but only first-order accurate in both space and time, making it unsuitable for resolving wave propagation accurately in this context.

Finite volume with MUSCL and RK3 is stable and robust, but the method is more suited for problems with shocks or discontinuities. For this smooth, periodic, linear problem, higher-order FD or spectral methods are preferable.
IMEX is robust for stiff problems, but the linearized shallow-water equations here are not stiff. The plan is stable and efficient, but the extra complexity is unnecessary; accuracy is moderate.
Spectral method with RK3 is accurate and efficient, but the lower spatial resolution (Nx=64) and lower time order compared to the RK4 spectral plan reduce its accuracy for this problem.
